name: srsRAN 4G
note: Open-source SDR 4G suite: srsUE (cell search, MIB/SIB receiver, MAC-LTE/RRC PCAP output), srsENB (a full eNodeB) and srsEPC. The reference open LTE stack on HackRF/bladeRF/USRP — passive receiver for Capture, and the basis for fake-eNB/IMSI-catcher research when transmitting under authorisation.

name: srsRAN Project
note: SRS's O-RAN 5G CU/DU solution (with 4G support). The newer codebase from the srsRAN team; for LTE cell-search/eNodeB work the srsRAN_4G suite is the directly applicable tool.

name: LTESniffer
note: Open-source LTE downlink/uplink eavesdropper: decodes PDCCH DCIs and the scheduled PDSCH/PUSCH of a cell, recovering RNTIs and per-UE scheduling information passively. Built for security research on a USRP-class SDR.

name: FALCON
note: Fast Analysis of LTE Control channels — built on srsRAN to blind-decode the entire PDCCH in real time, exposing every DCI/RNTI scheduling grant in a cell. A passive view of live control-channel activity and resource usage.

name: gr-lte
note: GNU Radio LTE receiver flowgraph: synchronisation, channel estimation and PBCH decoding implemented as composable DSP blocks. A learning/receiver toolkit for understanding the LTE downlink rather than a full SIB/PDCCH suite (last active ~2020).

name: Open5GS
note: Open-source C implementation of the EPC (and 5G Core): MME/HSS/SGW/PGW for LTE. Paired with srsENB it provides the core network behind a test/rogue eNodeB so you can provision test subscribers and drive attach/authentication/identity NAS procedures end-to-end.

name: OpenAirInterface (OAI)
note: Full open-source LTE/NR RAN and core reference stack (eNodeB/gNB, UE and EPC). A second independent platform — alongside srsRAN — for standing up a test/rogue eNodeB on a TX-capable SDR and for RRC/NAS signalling experiments and protocol fuzzing in an authorised lab.

name: Crocodile Hunter
note: EFF's 4G fake-eNodeB / IMSI-catcher detector: passively monitors LTE broadcast (PCI, EARFCN, SIBs) to fingerprint cell-site simulators (Stingray/Hailstorm class) by their anomalies. Built on srsRAN; the defensive counterpart used to validate that a rogue-eNB test is detectable.

name: srsRAN active IMSI catcher
note: A small research project that modifies srsRAN_4G's srsENB into an active IMSI catcher — stands up a fake base station that lures UEs and issues an identity request to extract the IMSI over the unauthenticated pre-AKA NAS exchange. A concrete worked example of the IMSI-catcher attack on srsRAN.

name: MobileInsight
note: Passive UE-side cellular analyzer: decodes the device's own LTE control-plane messages (RRC, NAS, paging, measurement reports) from a diagnostic feed, so you can observe paging/tracking and measurement behaviour and confirm the effect of an attack from the victim UE's perspective.

name: SIMCom SIM7600
spec: LTE Cat-4 modem · multi-band sub-6 GHz
note: Low-cost commercial SIMCom LTE Cat-4 modem (Qualcomm MDM-based). Reports serving- and neighbour-cell information through its own AT command set — AT+CPSI? returns the system mode, operator (MCC-MNC), LAC, Cell ID, band and EARFCN — a no-SDR way to read which cell a target is on and identify it. Because the baseband is Qualcomm and exposes a /dev/diag port, the same module can also feed QCSuper to capture raw cellular signalling.

name: QCSuper
note: Captures raw 2G/3G/4G (and, on some modems, 5G) radio frames from Qualcomm-based phones and modems via the /dev/diag DIAG interface, wraps them in GSMTAP and streams a live PCAP into Wireshark. Turns a cheap Qualcomm modem such as the SIM7600 into a passive cellular-signalling sniffer — an alternative to an SDR cell-search receiver.

name: Orbic RC400L hotspot
spec: LTE Cat-4 mobile hotspot · Qualcomm modem (/dev/diag)
note: Low-cost (~US$20–30) Qualcomm-based LTE mobile hotspot (also branded Kajeet RC400L) that exposes a /dev/diag diagnostic interface — the reference, best-tested hardware for running EFF's Rayhunter IMSI-catcher detector. The TP-Link M7350 is also supported; in principle any device with a Qualcomm modem exposing /dev/diag may work.

name: Rayhunter
note: EFF's IMSI-catcher / cell-site-simulator detector. Runs as firmware on a cheap Qualcomm-based mobile hotspot (Orbic RC400L, TP-Link M7350) and passively analyses the LTE control plane from the modem's /dev/diag feed, raising an alert on cell-site-simulator behaviour — unexpected identity/IMSI requests, downgrade attempts or suspicious tower configuration. The portable, low-cost blue-team counterpart to Crocodile Hunter for confirming whether a rogue station is present, with no SDR or host PC.
